source_note: Maria-style scenario · keyed line anchors on M.G. §10.1 verbatim opener. Distractors drawn from §10.4 P.B. + §11 GC-19/17/20.
stem: You're about to dial Maria — owner of a 2-store retail SMB. Companies House shows she opened the second store in January. Which opener does the work of M1?

choices.text: Hi Maria — do you have a moment to talk about your corporate spending?
choices.feedback: Generic. No signal, no diagnostic. Pattern GC-19 — Maria's bottom-quartile counterpart hung up at 0:08.

choices.text: I noticed you opened a second location 6 months ago — who's approving expense reports across both stores now?
choices.feedback: Correct. Specific signal (second location) + who-controls-what question. Pattern GC-01 — booked a demo at 14 minutes.

choices.text: Our card is cheaper than your bank's — worth 10 minutes?
choices.feedback: Fees argument is a pitch, not a diagnostic. Pattern GC-20 — call ended at 0:24.

choices.text: We help SMB owners get real-time visibility into their team spend — can I tell you more?
choices.feedback: Pitch-launch. Pattern GC-17 — buyer hung up at 0:22 because no diagnostic was asked.

source_note: Worked-example completion on Tom · §12.2 Series-B founder · drives variability vs Maria. Keyed answer pairs signal with who-controls-what in ≤ 15 words.
stem: Tom just closed a Series B (LinkedIn announcement last month, 22 FTE, 6 engineering hires posted). Which diagnostic best fits LO-M1.2 — signal + who-controls-what in ≤ 15 words?

choices.text: Congratulations on the Series B — that must be a huge milestone for the team. Would love to hear how it's going.
choices.feedback: Too long, no who-controls-what question. Compliment is not a diagnostic.

choices.text: Saw your Series B last month — who's owning per-employee card issuance now that engineering is scaling?
choices.feedback: Correct. 16 words, specific signal (Series B), who-controls-what (per-employee card issuance). Anchors on §10.2 L.D. and GC-06.

choices.text: We help fast-growing SaaS teams with corporate cards — sound like something you'd want to evaluate?
choices.feedback: Pitch-launch, no signal. Same anti-pattern as GC-17.
Closed-ended yes/no. No signal anchoring. Even if Tom says 'yes', you have nothing to acknowledge in M2.

source_note: Threshold-concept item · anchored on M.G. §10.1 verbatim ('Ask one good question and then shut up for 5 seconds'). Distractors are §10.4 P.B. anti-patterns.
stem: You ask your diagnostic. The buyer pauses for 6 seconds — no audible breath, no reply. What do you do?

choices.text: Rephrase the question more clearly so they understand it.
choices.feedback: You just took the silence away. §10.1 — 'Five seconds feels like an hour but it's where the deal happens.'

choices.text: Fill the gap with a quick benefit statement to keep momentum.
choices.feedback: This is the bottom-quartile reflex (§10.4 P.B.). Pitching into silence collapses the diagnostic.

choices.text: Wait. Let the buyer fill the silence.
choices.feedback: Correct. LO-M1.3 — tolerate the 5-second silence. The buyer is thinking; that's where the actual pain reveals itself.

choices.text: Ask if they're still on the line.
choices.feedback: Breaks the silence and signals discomfort. The buyer reads it as a pitch rep, not a peer.
